title: Alnylam — AHP — Thesis
html:
Forward-Looking Thesis: GIVLAARI / Acute Hepatic Porphyria

Purpose: 3-5 bets on where the AHP market is heading and what ZoomRx should be ready to advise on Time horizon: 12-24 months (through 2027) Confidence levels: High / Medium / Low

Thesis 1: The Addressable Market Will Expand Through Diagnostic Acceleration, Not Competitive Dynamics

Confidence: High

Bet: GIVLAARI's revenue growth over the next 24 months will be driven primarily by growth in the diagnosed AHP patient pool rather than by competitive share gains or new indications. Alnylam's investments in disease awareness, genetic cascade testing programs, and partnerships with diagnostic networks will begin to compress the 15-year average diagnostic delay.

Supporting evidence:

  • Population-level genetic studies show pathogenic AHP variants at 1 in 1,300-1,785 -- orders of magnitude higher than the ~1 in 100,000 clinical prevalence. The vast majority of symptomatic patients have never been diagnosed
  • GIVLAARI already has no real competitor for prophylactic therapy -- there is no share to take from a rival brand
  • 26% of AHP patients are initially misdiagnosed, and the average diagnostic journey spans 15 years. Even modest improvements here would materially expand the addressable market
  • ZoomRx ATU data shows 59-61% of patients in the US and Brazil are referred from other specialties (PCPs, GI) -- these referring physicians are the bottleneck. Education campaigns targeting non-specialists are the highest-ROI commercial investment
  • Alnylam's 2025 patient growth of ~15% YoY was driven by new patient starts, not share gains from hemin

What ZoomRx should prepare for:

  • Alnylam may request a study focused on referring physician awareness and diagnostic behaviors (PCPs, ER physicians, GI specialists outside porphyria centers)
  • ATU surveys may need to add a "referral journey" module tracking how patients reach porphyria specialists
  • Disease awareness campaign effectiveness tracking (pre/post educational intervention)

Risk to thesis: If diagnostic acceleration programs underperform and the diagnosed pool grows at only 5-10% per year, GIVLAARI will plateau at its current base, and Alnylam will deprioritize the brand relative to the TTR franchise

Thesis 2: The Chronicity Narrative Will Unlock Patient Types 4 and 5, Expanding GIVLAARI Beyond "Recurrent Attackers"

Confidence: High

Bet: The reframing of AHP from "episodic attack disease" to "chronic disease with biochemical toxicity" will expand GIVLAARI's use into Patient Types 4 (chronic symptoms, no recent attacks) and eventually Type 5 (biochemically active, asymptomatic). This will be the primary narrative Alnylam pushes to expand the indication's commercial scope without requiring a formal label change.

Supporting evidence:

  • US HCPs' top GIVLAARI treatment goal has shifted to "improve chronic symptoms" (ahead of "prevent attacks" in the Enthusiast segment)
  • Majority of US HCPs now view AHP as a predominantly chronic condition (Wave 4)
  • GIVLAARI preference is high for patients with chronic pain, peripheral neuropathy, and CKD -- conditions associated with sustained ALA/PBG elevation, not just attack frequency
  • The ENVISION trial showed that GIVLAARI reduces ALA/PBG levels by ~94%, addressing the root cause of both attacks and chronic toxicity
  • Payer access barriers are currently anchored on attack counts (4+ per year). Reframing to chronic disease management could open alternative payer pathways

What ZoomRx should prepare for:

  • Alnylam will likely push for ATU questions that probe treatment willingness in Patient Types 4 and 5 more deeply -- not just preference but actual prescribing behavior
  • Expect requests for real-world evidence on chronic symptom improvement in GIVLAARI patients (patient-reported outcomes, QoL data beyond clinical trials)
  • PET messaging may evolve to lead with chronic symptom data rather than attack reduction data
  • Potential request for a dedicated chronic-symptom-focused patient study or qualitative work with Type 4/5 patients

Risk to thesis: If payers refuse to cover GIVLAARI for patients without documented attack history (Types 4 and 5), the chronicity narrative will be academically compelling but commercially irrelevant. Real-world coverage data would clarify this risk.

Thesis 3: Brazil Will Require a Fundamentally Different Commercial Model -- and May Remain Structurally Stalled

Confidence: Medium-High

Bet: Brazil will not follow the US/Germany adoption curve for GIVLAARI. The ~45% of diagnosed patients not on consistent treatment, the cost/payer barriers, the proximity-to-care sensitivity, and the attack-focused (rather than chronicity-focused) disease perception create a structural access problem that standard commercial tactics cannot solve. Growth in Brazil requires policy-level or partnership-level interventions -- not just better messaging.

Supporting evidence:

  • Brazil ATU Wave 4: ~45% of patients not on consistent treatment due to cost/payer restrictions
  • 40% of Brazilian HCPs cite proximity to care as a significant factor in treatment decisions -- suggesting geographic access barriers
  • Brazilian HCPs are less likely to consider AHP events without medical intervention as true attacks -- a more conservative disease definition that narrows the treatable population
  • GIVLAARI patient share expected to remain flat (~26%) in the next 12 months -- no growth anticipated
  • Brazil is an outlier on disease perception: HCPs still prioritize attack prevention over LTC management
  • Patient refusal became a more prominent barrier in W4, possibly linked to cost burden and treatment fatigue

What ZoomRx should prepare for:

  • Alnylam may request a dedicated access/payer study for Brazil -- different methodology from the ATU
  • Possible request for a patient-journey study in Brazil to understand the structural barriers (referral patterns, geographic access, public vs. private payer dynamics)
  • May see Alnylam pursue partnerships with Brazilian healthcare systems for in-home administration programs
  • ATU sample size in Brazil (n=15) is a persistent limitation -- Alnylam may push for larger samples or qualitative depth

Risk to thesis: If Alnylam secures a favorable public formulary listing or implements a cost-sharing program in Brazil, the structural barriers could be partially bypassed. But this requires policy action, not commercial action.

Thesis 4: The PET and ATU Need to Converge -- Siloed Tracking Is Missing the Full Picture

Confidence: Medium

Bet: The current split between promotional tracking (PET) and disease/usage tracking (ATU) is creating blind spots. The PET captures what reps say and how HCPs respond, but it does not capture how those promotional interactions translate into actual prescribing behavior shifts over time. Meanwhile, the ATU captures behavior and beliefs but not the promotional context that shapes them. Alnylam will push for an integrated tracking approach -- or at minimum, cross-study linkage -- within the next 12 months.

Supporting evidence:

  • PET Q2 2023 showed all messages above industry average, NPS improving, high-impact details -- yet ATU Wave 4 (Oct 2024) still shows the Inexperienced segment (47% of HCPs) barely engaging with GIVLAARI. Promotional effectiveness does not translate into adoption in this segment
  • The disease perception shift captured in ATU Wave 4 (chronic vs. episodic framing) should have implications for message prioritization, but the most recent PET predates this shift by 15+ months
  • Germany has the highest GIVLAARI share but is not covered by the PET -- so we cannot assess whether German adoption is driven by promotional activity, structural factors, or clinical leadership
  • The HCP segmentation in the ATU (Enthusiasts/Trialists/Converters/Inexperienced) should inform which segments reps target and what messages they lead with -- but this segmentation is not reflected in PET design

What ZoomRx should prepare for:

  • A refreshed PET that incorporates the chronicity narrative and tests updated messaging
  • Potential request for an integrated tracker combining ATU and PET elements -- or at minimum, consistent HCP segmentation across both studies
  • Cross-referencing promotional exposure with actual prescribing behavior by segment

Risk to thesis: If Alnylam's market insights team prefers to maintain separate tracks for different internal stakeholders (commercial vs. medical affairs), integration may be resisted for organizational reasons rather than strategic ones.

Thesis 5: Off-Label Dosing and Adherence Signals Will Prompt Alnylam to Explore Dosing Optimization

Confidence: Medium-Low

Bet: The 14% of US HCPs administering GIVLAARI at different dosages (Wave 4), primarily in recurrent attackers and severe-history patients, signals that monthly dosing may be insufficient for a subset of patients. Combined with the 16% who don't consistently achieve the intended dosing schedule, this creates a case for Alnylam to explore dosing optimization -- potentially more frequent dosing or higher doses for refractory patients.

Supporting evidence:

  • 14% of US HCPs reported off-label dosing (ZoomRx has reported these cases to the appropriate agency)
  • Different dosing primarily observed in Patient Type 1 (recurrent attacks) and Type 2 (severe history)
  • Only 84% of HCPs achieve the intended dosing schedule
  • Long-term OLE data shows sustained efficacy, but some patients continue to experience breakthrough attacks
  • The AHP market is small enough that even modest subgroup data could support a dosing study

What ZoomRx should prepare for:

  • Potential request for a dosing-focused ad-hoc analysis from ATU data
  • Questions about breakthrough attack frequency and management in GIVLAARI-treated patients
  • If Alnylam pursues a dosing study, ZoomRx may be asked to help with study design or endpoint selection

Risk to thesis: The small patient numbers make formal dosing studies difficult. Alnylam may decide the commercial return does not justify the investment, especially given GIVLAARI's position in the rare disease portfolio vs. the TTR franchise's growth priority.

Summary: Where to Watch

# Thesis Confidence Timeframe Key Signal
1 Diagnostic acceleration drives growth High 12-24 months New patient start rate exceeds 15% YoY
2 Chronicity narrative expands treatable population High 12-18 months Type 4/5 GIVLAARI share grows; payer coverage beyond attack thresholds
3 Brazil structurally stalled Medium-High 12-24 months Brazil share remains flat or access study requested
4 PET and ATU convergence needed Medium 6-12 months Integrated study brief or refreshed PET post-ATU W4
5 Dosing optimization explored Medium-Low 18-24 months Off-label dosing rate increases or Alnylam initiates dosing study
